nodes.title: Avoid cheap risks under uncertainty
nodes.text: When evidence about a health exposure is genuinely uncertain, it is worth avoiding cheap, low-cost precautions against it even without definitive proof of harm.

nodes.title: Cardiovascular risk should dominate the verdict
nodes.text: Because cardiovascular disease is a leading cause of death, cardiovascular risk should be the dominant consideration in judging whether eggs are good for you, even when other nutritional properties are favorable.

nodes.title: Egg protein's high biological value supports child development
nodes.text: Egg protein has a biological value of 97%, the highest among common foods, providing all nine essential amino acids in optimal proportions for supporting skeletal and muscle development during childhood growth.

nodes.title: Athletes clear dietary cholesterol faster
nodes.text: Athletes and other highly physically active people typically exhibit greater lipid turnover and more efficient clearance of dietary cholesterol, driven by exercise-induced increases in lipoprotein lipase activity and HDL-mediated reverse cholesterol transport, which may blunt the LDL-raising effect of egg consumption seen in sedentary populations.

nodes.title: Eggs raise cardiovascular risk
nodes.text: Egg consumption meaningfully raises cardiovascular disease risk by increasing dietary cholesterol intake.

nodes.title: Eggs are neutral for most healthy adults
nodes.text: For most healthy adults, moderate egg consumption is nutritionally neutral and does not meaningfully raise cardiovascular disease risk.

nodes.title: Diabetic egg-CVD link is causal
nodes.text: The elevated egg-CVD association seen among people with diabetes is causal: eggs, or their cholesterol and nutrient load, directly worsen cardiovascular risk given diabetic physiology.

nodes.title: No CVD association in meta-analyses (general population)
nodes.text: Meta-analytic evidence shows no statistically significant association between egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease in the general adult population.

nodes.title: Saturated fat, not cholesterol, drives LDL
nodes.text: Dietary saturated fat, not dietary cholesterol intake itself, is the primary dietary driver of serum LDL cholesterol levels for most people.

nodes.title: Eggs support childhood brain development
nodes.text: Dietary cholesterol and choline supplied by eggs serve as building blocks for brain and nervous system development during childhood, giving eggs a comparatively larger developmental benefit in that life stage than in adulthood.
Egg allergy prevalence shapes population-level advice.

nodes.title: Dose-response analysis: no CVD risk gradient in general population
nodes.text: Dose-response meta-analytic evidence indicates no relationship between increasing egg consumption quantity and cardiovascular disease risk in the general adult population.
